title: Mexican Chilaquiles

ingredients.name: corn or flour tortillas, cut into wedges

subs.name: stale roti or chapati torn into pieces works as a wonderful base for this dish
skip the egg for a vegetarian version; shredded paneer or curd on top adds protein

ingredients.name: dried red chillies or red chilli powder

subs.name: 1.5 tsp red chilli powder gives the same heat if whole chillies are unavailable
crumbled paneer or a few tbsp of thick curd is fine

steps.text: Cut the tortillas into triangles or rough 5 cm squares. Heat 2 tbsp oil in a kadai over medium-high flame and shallow-fry the tortilla pieces in batches for 2–3 minutes until crisp and golden. Drain on paper and set aside.

Blend the tomatoes, onion, garlic and dried red chillies together into a smooth sauce. In the same kadai with a little more oil, pour in the blended sauce — it will spit, so stand back. Cook over medium heat for 5–6 minutes, stirring often, until the sauce deepens in colour and smell.

Season the sauce with cumin and salt, add the stock and stir. Tip in the fried tortilla pieces and fold them through the sauce gently — you want them coated but not broken. Simmer for 2–3 minutes until the tortillas have absorbed the sauce and softened to your preference. Remove from heat.
In a separate small frying pan, heat a thin film of oil and fry the eggs sunny-side up over medium flame until the whites are set but the yolks are still runny, about 2–3 minutes.

Divide the chilaquiles between two plates, set a fried egg on top of each and finish with curd, crumbled cheese and coriander leaves. Eat immediately — the tortillas continue to soften and the dish is best right off the pan.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep water away from hot oil, lower food gently, avoid overfilling the vessel and never leave frying unattended.
critical_checks: Use pasteurised eggs for preparations served raw or lightly cooked, especially for higher-risk people.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: tortilla → stale roti or chapati torn into pieces works as a wonderful base for this dish; egg → skip the egg for a vegetarian version; shredded paneer or curd on top adds protein; dried-red-chilli → 1.5 tsp red chilli powder gives the same heat if whole chillies are unavailable; cheese → crumbled paneer or a few tbsp of thick curd is fine
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Stale or day-old rotis are a perfect substitute for tortillas — tear them into rough triangles and shallow-fry or toast dry on a tawa until crisp before adding to the sauce. The sauce is basically a spiced tomato gravy that any Indian cook will feel at home making. The dish has a strong resemblance to the North Indian 'roti churi' concept — comfort food built around repurposing bread.
